title: When Can I Quit? Calculator
description: Calculate when you can realistically quit your job based on savings, expenses, and investment returns.
methodSummary: Time calculation uses compound interest formula solving for months at the given expected return.
assumptions: Assumes your expenses remain constant over time.
Net salary is estimated at 75% of gross for savings calculations.
insights: Every $100 you cut from your monthly expenses reduces your required 'Financial Independence' target by $30,000.
A 6-month buffer is often the 'minimum viable' runway to test a new career path or business idea.

faqs.a: It suggests that once you have 25 times your annual expenses saved, you can potentially live off the returns indefinitely (The 4% Rule).
6 months provides most professionals enough time to find a comparable job if a new venture fails, reducing the risk of 'quitting'.
Yes, recurring side income directly reduces the amount you need to save from your main salary to reach your goal.
It is a mathematical projection. Real-world inflation and market volatility will impact the actual date.

faqs.q: What if my expenses change after I quit?
faqs.a: Enter the amount you expect to spend *after* quitting to get a more accurate 'Safe Quit' target.
No, returns fluctuate. Use a conservative number (like 4-5%) for more realistic long-term planning.

richContent:
The Math Behind Quitting: When Is It Financially Safe to Leave?

Quitting a job is one of the highest-stakes financial decisions most people make, yet most do it based on emotion rather than math. The quit calculator answers a simple question: given your current savings rate, expenses, and investment returns, how many months until you have enough financial cushion to leave safely?

The tool calculates two milestones. The first is your safety buffer — six months of expenses, enough to job-search without panic. The second is your financial independence number — 25 times your annual expenses, the point at which investment returns can theoretically cover your costs indefinitely (the 4% rule).

Why Expenses Matter More Than Income

The most powerful lever in this calculation is not your salary — it is your spending. Every $100 you cut from monthly expenses does three things simultaneously: it increases your monthly savings rate, it reduces the size of your required safety buffer, and it lowers your financial independence target by $30,000. A person earning $80,000 who spends $3,000 per month reaches financial safety far faster than someone earning $150,000 who spends $8,000 per month.

The 6-Month Safety Buffer

Six months of expenses is the minimum viable runway for most career transitions. It gives you time to job-search without accepting the first offer out of desperation, test a business idea, or retrain for a new field. Below this threshold, quitting is a gamble rather than a calculated risk.

Side Income as an Accelerator

Even modest side income dramatically accelerates your timeline. $500 per month in freelance or project work effectively increases your savings rate and provides proof of concept for an independent career. Enter it in the calculator to see how it shifts your safety milestone forward.

The Opportunity Cost of Staying

This calculator shows when you can quit, but the decision also involves the cost of staying. If your current role is limiting career growth, causing burnout, or below market rate, every month you delay the switch has its own price. Use the Burnout Cost Calculator to quantify that side of the equation.
